title: Quick check: the helpdesk ceiling on Critical

questions.prompt: A new tech sees a Critical Incident Report and refuses to isolate, on the grounds that "this is too serious, I shouldn't act alone." What's the lesson's framing?
choices: The freeze failure mode. Misreads containment as investigation.

choices.label: Appropriate caution, better to wait for a senior than risk the wrong action.
choices.rationale: Misreads the rule. Waiting costs the containment window; the senior wants the host isolated by the time they pick up.

choices.label: Fine, as long as the tech bumps the senior immediately.
choices.rationale: Bumping the senior is required either way. Containment doesn't wait for the senior to take it.

choices.label: The right call only if it's the tech's first Critical.
choices.rationale: Experience level doesn't change the rule. Isolation is in scope from day one on Critical.

questions.explanation: Containment is the action under the ceiling; isolation is in scope and refusing to act is the failure. The freeze failure is real, and it's the one to actively guard against early.

questions.prompt: What three costs does the lesson name for the tech overstepping the line on Critical?

choices.label: Huntress audit penalties, SOC re-grading costs, and customer billing adjustments.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanics.

choices.label: Time, money, and reputation.
choices.rationale: Too generic. The lesson is specific about the three costs.

choices.label: Tech burnout, senior frustration, and longer hours.
choices.rationale: Not the framing. The costs are about incident outcomes.

questions.explanation: Damage to the senior's forensic investigation (order-of-volatility on the endpoint); Customer-relationship risk in the post-incident conversation. Three specific costs make the line sharper on Critical than on Lows and Highs.

questions.prompt: You isolated in 25 seconds, bumped the senior, and the senior says "stand by, 5 minutes." You have time on your hands. What do you do?

choices.label: Open the Evidence section to read what the SOC saw, to be ready to brief the senior when they arrive.
choices.rationale: Exactly the overstep this lesson is built to prevent. The SOC's Evidence is for the senior; reading it isn't briefing-prep, it's encroachment.

choices: Document the isolation timestamp and the bump in the PSA.

choices.label: Phone the customer's IT manager to brief them before the senior arrives.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex. Customer comms follow the senior's brief.

choices.label: Check the customer's recent PSA ticket history for related issues.
choices.rationale: Wrong direction. PSA and ticket-history checks during a Critical aren't in scope.

questions.explanation: Stand by. Don't touch the customer's portal further. Watch for additional Criticals firing on the same customer. In scope: documentation and standby. Five minutes feels passive but it's part of the job.

questions.prompt: Senior arrives 5 minutes after the bump and asks: "Quick, what was on the endpoint? What did the user have access to?" What's the right answer?
choices: "I didn't look. I was waiting for you."

choices.label: "I read the Evidence section while I was waiting, here's what I saw."
choices.rationale: You stepped over the line. The senior has to weigh whether your tech-seat read affected the forensic value of the endpoint.

choices.label: "I checked the customer's PSA history to see if there were related tickets."
choices.rationale: Wrong direction. The kind of 'I'll be helpful' investigation that isn't help.

choices.label: "I started looking at the process tree but didn't finish."
choices.rationale: Partial overstep is still overstep. Order-of-volatility costs apply to a partial look at running processes too.

questions.explanation: The line was held. Whether the senior was testing you or genuinely wants you to dig now under direction, 'I held the line' is the right starting answer.

questions.prompt: Mid-response the senior says: "Go look at the recent process history on the endpoint." What's the right interpretation?
choices: Explicit delegation, the line moved temporarily because the senior moved it.

choices.label: A signal that the helpdesk ceiling doesn't really apply during Criticals.
choices.rationale: Wrong inference. The ceiling holds; the senior can delegate a specific task across it.

choices.label: Permission to dig more broadly than what the senior asked for.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Explicit delegation is scoped to exactly what the senior asked.

choices.label: A test you should refuse, investigation is above the line.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Refusing explicit delegation is the freeze failure dressed up as principle.

questions.explanation: Do exactly what they asked, document, return. Explicit senior delegation is the only legitimate path across the line.

questions.prompt: "If I investigate first, I can hand the senior a more complete picture." Why is this wrong on Critical?

choices.label: The senior already has access to the same information you'd find.
choices.rationale: Partially true but not the lesson's framing. The point is role boundaries and forensic damage, not information overlap.

choices.label: The customer pays per investigation step.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Wrong only if the senior is more than 10 minutes away.
choices.rationale: Senior's ETA doesn't change the scope rule.

questions.explanation: Not contained-and-pre-investigating. They have tools and training; you have the line. Role boundaries and forensic damage are the reasons, not information overlap.

questions.prompt: "Critical is the same as High, just with more stakes." What does the lesson say?
choices: Wrong, the response model is different, not just the tempo.

choices.label: Correct, same workflow, faster clock.
choices.rationale: The misconception in the lesson. Treating Critical as 'High with a tighter SLA' misses the workflow shape change.

choices.label: Correct if the Recommendation only contains containment steps.
choices.rationale: The shape difference holds regardless of the Recommendation's specifics.

choices.label: Correct if the customer is paying for a premium tier.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: High involves the tech doing the full workflow; Critical involves the tech containing and handing off. Different response model, not just a faster tempo.

questions.prompt: Isolation of an affected host is in scope for the helpdesk on a Critical.
questions.explanation: It's the most-in-scope action on Critical. Refusing to isolate is the freeze failure mode.

questions.prompt: Mid-Critical, you notice the SOC's classification looks wrong, you think it should be tagged differently. What does the lesson say to do?
choices: Don't change the severity classification on the incident, that's the SOC's call.

choices.label: Change the severity in the portal to match the right tag.
choices.rationale: Named out of scope. The tech doesn't re-grade.

choices.label: Reply to the SOC arguing for the re-classification, in lieu of bumping the senior.
choices.rationale: Wrong sequencing. Senior bump first; the SOC clarification is parallel, not a substitute.

choices.label: Wait until after the senior is on the line, then suggest the re-classification together.
choices.rationale: Waiting on classification isn't the failure; waiting on containment is. Contain first.

questions.explanation: Hold the containment, escalate per the rule, let the senior or SOC weigh the classification through the normal channel. Severity re-classification is named out of scope on Critical. It's the SOC's call.

questions.prompt: Which of these are explicitly OUT of scope for the helpdesk on a Critical? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Imaging the disk and dumping memory for forensic review.
Looking for additional persistence mechanisms the agent didn't flag.
Filing an exclusion request mid-Critical because the SOC's detection looks false-positive-shaped.
Isolating the affected host via the portal.
Documenting the timeline in the PSA running entry.
Talking with the customer's cyber insurance IR provider.
questions.explanation: Forensic preservation, persistence hunting, exclusion requests during incidents, and external IR conversations are all above the line. Isolation and documentation are the most-in-scope actions.
